schools.summary: Israel's arrival at Sinai in the third month: 'in the third month, when the children of Israel were gone forth out of the land of Egypt, the same day came they into the wilderness of Sinai' — Israel arriving at the wilderness of Sinai in the third month after the Exodus (about the time of Pentecost, fifty days from the Passover, the journey to the mount of God, Exodus 3:12, where Moses was promised they would serve God, the providence, the people brought to the place of the covenant); the time (the third month, the new moon, or the same day) — the arrival at Sinai about fifty days from the Exodus (the witnesses noting the providential timing, the law given at Sinai about the season later kept as Pentecost, the feast of weeks, the Spirit given at Pentecost, Acts 2, the law written on the heart, a contrast and fulfillment); 'came they into the wilderness of Sinai' (the wilderness of Sinai, the mount of God, Horeb, Exodus 3:1, the place of the covenant and the law, vv.3-25; ch. 20, the providence, Israel brought to Sinai to receive the law, the covenant established) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, JFB, Wesley.

themes: In the third month, when the children of Israel were gone forth out of the land of Egypt, the same day came they into the wilderness of Sinai
Israel's arrival at Sinai in the third month
the mount of God, the place of the covenant and the law (Exodus 3:1, 12)
the providential timing - the law at Sinai, the Spirit at Pentecost (Acts 2)

schools.summary: Israel encamping before Mount Sinai: 'for they were departed from Rephidim, and were come to the desert of Sinai, and had pitched in the wilderness; and there Israel camped before the mount' — Israel, having left Rephidim (the place of the water and the battle with Amalek, ch. 17), coming to the desert of Sinai and camping before the mount (Sinai/Horeb, the encampment at the holy mount, the place of the covenant, the people gathered before the mount of God); the desert of Sinai (the wilderness before the mount) — the encampment (the gathering of the people); 'there Israel camped before the mount' (Israel before Mount Sinai, the mount of God, Exodus 3:1; 19:1, the camp at the foot of the holy mount, the providence, the people gathered before the mount where God would descend, vv.16-20, the law given, ch. 20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: For they were departed from Rephidim, and were come to the desert of Sinai, and had pitched in the wilderness; and there Israel camped before the mount
Israel encamping before Mount Sinai
the camp at the foot of the holy mount (Exodus 3:1; 19:1)
the people gathered where God would descend and give the law (vv.16-20)

schools.summary: Moses going up to God and the LORD's call from the mount: 'and Moses went up unto God, and the LORD called unto him out of the mountain, saying, Thus shalt thou say to the house of Jacob, and tell the children of Israel' — Moses going up the mount (toward God), and the LORD calling to him from the mountain (the mediator going up to God, Moses the go-between, ascending to receive God's word, the call, God speaking from the mount, the office of Moses, the mediator of the covenant, Galatians 3:19); Moses going up to God (the mediator, ascending the mount) — the office (Moses the channel between God and the people, the going-up to receive the word); 'Thus shalt thou say to the house of Jacob, and tell the children of Israel' (the message for Israel, the house of Jacob, the children of Israel, the covenant proposal, vv.4-6, the providence, the word that Moses would carry down to the people, the covenant offered) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Moses went up unto God, and the LORD called unto him out of the mountain, saying, Thus shalt thou say to the house of Jacob, and tell the children of Israel
Moses going up to God and the LORD's call from the mount
Moses the mediator of the covenant, ascending to receive God's word (Galatians 3:19)
the covenant proposal for Israel (vv.4-6)

schools.summary: God bearing Israel on eagles' wings: 'ye have seen what I did unto the Egyptians, and how I bare you on eagles' wings, and brought you unto myself' — God appealing to what Israel had seen (the plagues, the Red Sea, the deliverance, the witnessed works, the ground of the covenant, God's prior grace, the appeal to experience, the redemption already wrought); the great image — 'how I bare you on eagles' wings, and brought you unto myself': God bearing Israel as on eagles' wings (the tender, mighty, protecting care of God in the deliverance, the witnesses noting the figure, the eagle stirring up her nest, bearing her young on her wings, Deuteronomy 32:11-12, 'as an eagle... beareth them on her wings: so the LORD alone did lead him' — the eagle's strong, swift, lofty flight, the speed and power of the deliverance, the eagle bearing her young on her wings, not in her talons, but on her back, the tender, protective care, sheltering them from below, the lesson — God's mighty and tender care of his people, carrying them out of Egypt, over every danger, swiftly and safely, the parental love of God, bearing his people as an eagle her young, Isaiah 63:9, 'in his love and in his pity he redeemed them... he bare them, and carried them'); 'and brought you unto myself' (the end of the deliverance, God bringing Israel to himself, to the mount, into covenant, into his service and fellowship, the redemption's purpose, not merely out of Egypt, but unto God, into communion with him, the providence, the people brought to God, to be his own, vv.5-6, a type of the redemption that brings us to God, 1 Peter 3:18, 'that he might bring us to God') — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley, Ellicott.

debates.question: What does 'I bare you on eagles' wings' signify?

sides.position: The mighty, tender, protecting care of God in the deliverance (Deuteronomy 32:11-12; Isaiah 63:9) - the eagle bearing her young on her wings, the swift and safe redemption; 'brought you unto myself' - the end of the redemption, to bring Israel to God, into covenant and fellowship (1 Peter 3:18)

themes: Ye have seen what I did unto the Egyptians, and how I bare you on eagles' wings, and brought you unto myself
God bearing Israel on eagles' wings (the crux of the tender deliverance)
the mighty, tender, protecting care of God (Deuteronomy 32:11-12; Isaiah 63:9)
'brought you unto myself' - the redemption that brings his people to God (1 Peter 3:18)

schools.summary: The covenant condition and Israel as God's peculiar treasure: 'now therefore, if ye will obey my voice indeed, and keep my covenant, then ye shall be a peculiar treasure unto me above all people: for all the earth is mine' — God setting the covenant condition: if Israel would obey his voice and keep his covenant, they would be his peculiar treasure (the covenant terms, obedience the condition of the blessing, the engagement, the covenant of Sinai, the people called to obedience); 'a peculiar treasure unto me above all people' — Israel God's 'peculiar treasure' (Hebrew segullah, a special possession, a treasured property, a jewel) above all people (the election, Israel chosen, set apart, God's own special people, Deuteronomy 7:6; 14:2; 26:18; Psalm 135:4, the lesson — God's people his treasured possession, the church his jewels, Malachi 3:17, 'they shall be mine... in that day when I make up my jewels,' the peculiar people, Titus 2:14; 1 Peter 2:9, 'a peculiar people,' the value God sets on his own); 'for all the earth is mine' (the ground of the choice, God, who owns all the earth, chose Israel for his own, not from need, but of grace, the sovereign election, choosing one nation though all are his, the sovereignty, all the earth God's, yet Israel his special treasure, the providence, the free, sovereign choice of grace) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Now therefore, if ye will obey my voice indeed, and keep my covenant, then ye shall be a peculiar treasure unto me above all people: for all the earth is mine
the covenant condition and Israel as God's peculiar treasure
Israel God's special possession, his jewels (Deuteronomy 7:6; Malachi 3:17; 1 Peter 2:9)
chosen of sovereign grace, though all the earth is God's

schools.summary: Israel called a kingdom of priests and a holy nation: 'and ye shall be unto me a kingdom of priests, and an holy nation. These are the words which thou shalt speak unto the children of Israel' — the title — 'ye shall be unto me a kingdom of priests, and an holy nation': God designing Israel to be a kingdom of priests and a holy nation (the witnesses expounding — a 'kingdom of priests,' a royal priesthood, all the people in a sense priests, near to God, offering spiritual service, mediating between God and the nations, the whole nation consecrated to God's service, though the Aaronic priesthood was a special order within, the lesson — the priestly calling of God's people, the church a royal priesthood, the fulfillment in the New Testament, 1 Peter 2:9, 'ye are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, an holy nation, a peculiar people'; Revelation 1:6, 'hath made us kings and priests unto God'; 5:10; 20:6, the access of all believers to God, the priesthood of all the faithful, offering the sacrifices of praise, Hebrews 13:15; Romans 12:1, the nation set apart for God's worship and service); 'an holy nation' (Israel a nation set apart, consecrated, holy, separated unto God from the nations, the holiness, the people called to be holy, as God is holy, Leviticus 11:44-45; 19:2; 1 Peter 1:15-16, the separation unto God, the holy nation, distinct from the world, dedicated to God's service); 'these are the words which thou shalt speak unto the children of Israel' (the covenant words for Israel, the high calling, the priestly and holy nation, the providence, the calling that Israel would receive, vv.7-8, the type of the church, the royal priesthood, the holy nation, 1 Peter 2:9) — Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'a kingdom of priests, and an holy nation' signify?

sides.position: The priestly and consecrated calling of God's people, fulfilled in the church (1 Peter 2:9, 'a royal priesthood, an holy nation'; Revelation 1:6; 5:10) - the priesthood of all believers, the access to God and the offering of spiritual sacrifices (Hebrews 13:15; Romans 12:1); the holy nation set apart unto God (Leviticus 11:44-45; 1 Peter 1:15-16)

themes: And ye shall be unto me a kingdom of priests, and an holy nation. These are the words which thou shalt speak unto the children of Israel
Israel called a kingdom of priests and a holy nation (the crux of the priestly people)
the priestly calling fulfilled in the church, the royal priesthood (1 Peter 2:9; Revelation 1:6; 5:10)
the holy nation set apart unto God (Leviticus 11:44-45; 1 Peter 1:15-16)

schools.summary: Moses laying God's words before the elders: 'and Moses came and called for the elders of the people, and laid before their faces all these words which the LORD commanded him' — Moses coming down (from the mount, v.3) and calling the elders (the representatives of the people), laying before them all the words God had commanded (the covenant proposal, vv.4-6, the faithful delivery, Moses declaring all God's words, keeping nothing back, the mediator's office, carrying God's word to the people through their elders); the elders (the heads, the representatives of the people) — the assembly (the people addressed through their elders); 'laid before their faces all these words which the LORD commanded him' (Moses declaring all God's words, the whole covenant proposal, faithfully, the fidelity of the mediator, keeping back nothing, Acts 20:27, declaring all the counsel of God, the providence, the covenant offered to the people, their answer, v.8) — JFB, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Moses came and called for the elders of the people, and laid before their faces all these words which the LORD commanded him
Moses laying God's words before the elders
the faithful mediator declaring all God's word, keeping nothing back (Acts 20:27)
the covenant offered to the people through their elders

schools.summary: The people's promise to obey the covenant: 'and all the people answered together, and said, All that the LORD hath spoken we will do. And Moses returned the words of the people unto the LORD' — the people, with one voice, promising to do all the LORD had spoken (the covenant accepted, the people engaging to obey, the unanimous consent, the whole people, together, the engagement, the vow of obedience, repeated, Exodus 24:3, 7); the people's vow (to do all God commanded) — the witnesses weighing it: the promise sincere in its way, yet made in self-confidence (the people not knowing their own weakness, soon to break the covenant, the golden calf, ch. 32; Deuteronomy 5:27-29, 'O that there were such an heart in them,' the lesson — the covenant of works, do this and live, that the people could not keep, driving to the need of grace, the law a schoolmaster to Christ, Galatians 3:24, the engagement that exposed the weakness of the flesh); 'Moses returned the words of the people unto the LORD' (Moses carrying the people's answer back to God, the mediator going between, up and down the mount, the office, Moses the go-between, the covenant ratified through him, the providence, the covenant engaged, the preparation for the giving of the law, vv.9-25) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: How is the people's 'All that the LORD hath spoken we will do' to be regarded?

sides.position: Sincere in its way, yet made in self-confidence - the people not knowing their own weakness, soon to break the covenant (ch. 32; Deuteronomy 5:27-29); the covenant of works they could not keep, driving to the need of grace (the law a schoolmaster to Christ, Galatians 3:24)

themes: And all the people answered together, and said, All that the LORD hath spoken we will do. And Moses returned the words of the people unto the LORD
the people's promise to obey the covenant
the vow of obedience, sincere yet self-confident, soon broken (Exodus 24:3; Deuteronomy 5:27-29)
the covenant of works driving to the need of grace (Galatians 3:24)

schools.summary: God coming in the cloud to confirm Moses' mediation: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Lo, I come unto thee in a thick cloud, that the people may hear when I speak with thee, and believe thee for ever. And Moses told the words of the people unto the LORD' — God announcing he would come in a thick cloud (the visible token of his presence, the cloud veiling his glory) and speak with Moses in the people's hearing (the theophany announced, God's descent in the cloud, vv.16-20, the purpose, that the people might hear God speaking with Moses); the people hearing God speak with Moses (the audible confirmation) — the witness to Moses' mission (the people made to hear God's voice); 'and believe thee for ever' (the purpose: that the people might believe Moses for ever, his mission confirmed by hearing God speak with him, the establishing of the mediator, Moses' authority confirmed, that the people might trust him and his word, the law, the providence, the theophany confirming Moses as God's prophet, a type of Christ, the Mediator confirmed by the voice from heaven, Matthew 17:5, 'hear ye him') — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Lo, I come unto thee in a thick cloud, that the people may hear when I speak with thee, and believe thee for ever
God coming in the cloud to confirm Moses' mediation
the people to hear God speak with Moses, confirming his mission (vv.16-20)
the mediator established, a type of Christ confirmed from heaven (Matthew 17:5)

schools.summary: The command to sanctify the people for God's coming: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Go unto the people, and sanctify them to day and to morrow, and let them wash their clothes' — God commanding Moses to sanctify (consecrate, set apart, prepare) the people two days, and to have them wash their clothes (the preparation for God's coming, the people made ready to meet God, vv.11, the consecration, the outward washing a sign of inward purity); the sanctifying (the consecration, the setting apart, the preparation for the holy meeting) — the readiness required to approach God (the lesson, the need of holiness to draw near to God, Hebrews 12:14, 'without which no man shall see the Lord'); 'let them wash their clothes' (the washing of the clothes, the outward cleansing, a sign of the inward purity required to meet God, the lesson, the cleansing needed to approach God, the type of the washing of regeneration, Titus 3:5, the garments washed in the blood of the Lamb, Revelation 7:14, the providence, the preparation for the theophany, vv.11, 16) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Go unto the people, and sanctify them to day and to morrow, and let them wash their clothes
the command to sanctify the people for God's coming
the consecration and cleansing required to meet God (Hebrews 12:14)
the washing a type of the washing of regeneration (Titus 3:5; Revelation 7:14)

schools.summary: The LORD's coming down on the third day: 'and be ready against the third day: for the third day the LORD will come down in the sight of all the people upon mount Sinai' — the people to be ready by the third day, when the LORD would come down on Mount Sinai in the sight of all (the appointed day, the theophany, vv.16-20, the descent of God, in the sight of all the people, the great meeting, God coming down to the mount); the people to be ready (prepared, sanctified) by the third day — the appointed time (the preparation completed); 'the LORD will come down in the sight of all the people upon mount Sinai' (the LORD coming down on Sinai, the theophany, the descent in fire and cloud, vv.16-18, the visible manifestation, in the sight of all the people, the providence, God descending to give the law, the awful majesty of the coming, Psalm 68:8; Habakkuk 3:3-6) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And be ready against the third day: for the third day the LORD will come down in the sight of all the people upon mount Sinai
the LORD's coming down on the third day
God descending in awful majesty to give the law (vv.16-20; Psalm 68:8)
the visible manifestation in the sight of all the people

schools.summary: The bounds set about the mount: 'and thou shalt set bounds unto the people round about, saying, Take heed to yourselves, that ye go not up into the mount, or touch the border of it: whosoever toucheth the mount shall be surely put to death' — Moses to set bounds (a barrier, a limit) around the mount, warning the people not to go up or even touch the border (the separation, the people kept at a distance from the holy mount, the fencing of the holy, the mount made sacred by God's presence, not to be approached); the unapproachable holiness — the people forbidden even to touch the mount (the witnesses noting the lesson — the terror and unapproachableness of God's holiness under the law, the people kept at a distance, the mount that 'might be touched' yet must not be, the law a ministration of distance and dread, Hebrews 12:18-21, 'ye are not come unto the mount that might be touched... and so terrible was the sight, that Moses said, I exceedingly fear and quake,' the contrast with the gospel, the access under grace, Hebrews 12:22-24, 'ye are come unto mount Sion... and to Jesus the mediator,' the new and living way, Hebrews 10:19-22, the rent veil, Matthew 27:51, the law that kept sinners at a distance, the gospel that brings near, the holiness of God that the sinner cannot approach but through the Mediator); 'whosoever toucheth the mount shall be surely put to death' (the death penalty for touching the mount, the severity of the law, the holiness that destroys the presumptuous, vv.13; Hebrews 12:20, 'if so much as a beast touch the mountain, it shall be stoned,' the terror of the law, the providence, the bounds teaching the distance between the holy God and sinful man, until the Mediator) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What do the bounds about the mount teach?

sides.position: The terror and unapproachableness of God's holiness under the law (Hebrews 12:18-21) - the people kept at a distance, the mount not to be touched on pain of death (Hebrews 12:20); the contrast with the gospel access (Hebrews 12:22-24; 10:19-22), the law keeping sinners at a distance, the gospel bringing near through the Mediator

themes: And thou shalt set bounds unto the people round about... that ye go not up into the mount, or touch the border of it: whosoever toucheth the mount shall be surely put to death
the bounds set about the mount (the crux of the unapproachable holiness)
the terror and distance of God's holiness under the law (Hebrews 12:18-21; 12:20)
the contrast with the gospel access through the Mediator (Hebrews 12:22-24; 10:19-22)

schools.summary: The severity of the bound and the trumpet's call: 'there shall not an hand touch it, but he shall surely be stoned, or shot through; whether it be beast or man, it shall not live: when the trumpet soundeth long, they shall come up to the mount' — whoever (or whatever) touched the mount to be stoned or shot through (not touched by hand, lest the executioners be defiled, the offender killed at a distance, the severity, man or beast, none to live, Hebrews 12:20, the terror of the holiness, the mount so sacred that even a beast touching it must die); the death of man or beast (the inviolable holiness of the mount) — the terror of the law (the unapproachable holiness, Hebrews 12:20); 'when the trumpet soundeth long, they shall come up to the mount' (the trumpet, the signal, by which the people might approach, only when summoned, and only to the bounds, not into the mount, the appointed approach, the people drawing near at God's call, by the trumpet, the herald of the theophany, vv.16, 19, the providence, the trumpet a type of the call of God, the last trump, 1 Corinthians 15:52; 1 Thessalonians 4:16) — JFB, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: There shall not an hand touch it, but he shall surely be stoned, or shot through; whether it be beast or man, it shall not live: when the trumpet soundeth long, they shall come up to the mount
the severity of the bound and the trumpet's call
the inviolable holiness, man or beast touching it must die (Hebrews 12:20)
the people approaching only at God's call, by the trumpet (1 Corinthians 15:52)

schools.summary: Moses sanctifying the people as commanded: 'and Moses went down from the mount unto the people, and sanctified the people; and they washed their clothes' — Moses coming down and sanctifying (consecrating, preparing) the people as God had commanded (v.10), and they washing their clothes (the carrying-out of the command, the preparation completed, the people made ready for the theophany, vv.16, the consecration); Moses sanctifying the people (consecrating, preparing them, as commanded, v.10) — the obedience (the people prepared); 'they washed their clothes' (the outward washing, the sign of the inward purity, v.10, the preparation completed, the providence, the people made ready to meet God, vv.16, the cleansing required for the holy meeting) — Gill.

themes: And Moses went down from the mount unto the people, and sanctified the people; and they washed their clothes
Moses sanctifying the people as commanded
the preparation completed, the people made ready for the theophany (v.10)
the cleansing required for the holy meeting with God

schools.summary: The charge of purity before meeting God: 'and he said unto the people, Be ready against the third day: come not at your wives' — Moses charging the people to be ready by the third day, and to abstain from their wives (conjugal relations) in the interval (the charge of purity, the abstinence a part of the consecration, the setting apart for the holy meeting, the preparation, the bodily purity a sign of the reverence and holiness required to meet God); the charge to be ready (by the third day, the appointed time, v.11) — the preparation (the people made ready); 'come not at your wives' (the temporary abstinence, a part of the consecration, the lawful use of marriage set aside for a season of special devotion, 1 Corinthians 7:5, 'that ye may give yourselves to... prayer,' the reverence, the bodily purity for the holy meeting, the setting apart unto God, the providence, the people prepared in body and spirit to meet God, vv.16) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And he said unto the people, Be ready against the third day: come not at your wives
the charge of purity before meeting God
the abstinence a part of the consecration (1 Corinthians 7:5)
the bodily purity and reverence required for the holy meeting

schools.summary: The awful theophany at Sinai: 'and it came to pass on the third day in the morning, that there were thunders and lightnings, and a thick cloud upon the mount, and the voice of the trumpet exceeding loud; so that all the people that was in the camp trembled' — the awful manifestation — on the third day, the LORD coming down on Sinai with thunders and lightnings, a thick cloud, and the loud, ever-louder trumpet, so that the people trembled (the witnesses noting the terror of the theophany — the dreadful majesty of God descending to give the law, the thunders, lightnings, cloud, fire, smoke, trumpet, earthquake, vv.16-18, the lesson — the terror of the law, the law given amid dread and majesty, to show the holiness and justice of God, and the terror of sin, Hebrews 12:18-21, 'blackness, and darkness, and tempest, and the sound of a trumpet, and the voice of words,' the contrast with the gospel, Sinai's terror against Zion's grace, Hebrews 12:22-24, the law a ministration of death and condemnation, 2 Corinthians 3:7-9, given in terror, that the gospel might be received in grace, the awful holiness and majesty of the Lawgiver); the trumpet (the supernatural, ever-louder trumpet, v.19, not of human making, the herald of God's coming) — the awful summons (the sound that made the people tremble, a type of the trump of God, the last day, 1 Thessalonians 4:16; Hebrews 12:19); 'all the people that was in the camp trembled' (the people trembling, in terror at the manifestation, the dread of the holy God, the providence, the terror that prepared the people to receive the law, the fear of God, Exodus 20:18-20, the law that worketh wrath, Romans 4:15) — Calvin, Wesley, Henry, Gill.

debates.question: What does the theophany at Sinai teach?

sides.position: The terror of the law - the dreadful majesty of God descending to give the law (thunders, lightnings, cloud, trumpet, fire, smoke), the terror that made the people tremble (Hebrews 12:18-21); the law a ministration of death and terror (2 Corinthians 3:7-9), in awful contrast with the gospel of grace (Hebrews 12:22-24)

themes: And it came to pass on the third day in the morning, that there were thunders and lightnings, and a thick cloud upon the mount, and the voice of the trumpet exceeding loud; so that all the people that was in the camp trembled
the awful theophany at Sinai (the crux of the terror of the law)
the dreadful majesty of God giving the law, the people trembling (Hebrews 12:18-21)
the law a ministration of death, in contrast with the gospel of grace (2 Corinthians 3:7-9; Hebrews 12:22-24)

schools.summary: Moses bringing the people to meet God at the mount: 'and Moses brought forth the people out of the camp to meet with God; and they stood at the nether part of the mount' — Moses leading the people out of the camp to meet God, and they standing at the foot (nether part) of the mount (the bringing to God, the people set before the holy mount, the meeting, the people gathered to receive God's word, the standing at the foot, within the bounds, not on the mount, vv.12); Moses bringing the people to meet God (the gathering before the mount) — the solemn meeting (the people brought to God); 'they stood at the nether part of the mount' (the people at the foot of the mount, within the bounds, v.12, the awful nearness, yet kept at a distance, the providence, the people gathered to hear God, the giving of the law, ch. 20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And Moses brought forth the people out of the camp to meet with God; and they stood at the nether part of the mount
Moses bringing the people to meet God at the mount
the people gathered before the holy mount to hear God's word
standing at the foot, within the bounds (v.12)

schools.summary: Mount Sinai in fire and smoke at God's descent: 'and mount Sinai was altogether on a smoke, because the LORD descended upon it in fire: and the smoke thereof ascended as the smoke of a furnace, and the whole mount quaked greatly' — the whole mount wrapped in smoke because the LORD descended on it in fire, the smoke rising like a furnace, the whole mount quaking (the awful manifestation, the fire the token of God's presence and holiness, Deuteronomy 4:11-12, 24, 'the LORD thy God is a consuming fire,' the descent in fire, the majesty and terror of God); the LORD descending in fire (the token of his presence, holiness, and consuming justice, Hebrews 12:29, 'our God is a consuming fire') — the awful manifestation (the holiness that consumes); 'the smoke thereof ascended as the smoke of a furnace, and the whole mount quaked greatly' (the smoke like a furnace, the mount quaking, the earthquake, Psalm 68:8; 114:7, 'tremble, thou earth, at the presence of the Lord'; Habakkuk 3:6, 10, the terror of the theophany, the very earth trembling at God's coming, the providence, the awful majesty of the Lawgiver, Hebrews 12:18-21, 26) — Calvin, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And mount Sinai was altogether on a smoke, because the LORD descended upon it in fire... and the whole mount quaked greatly
Mount Sinai in fire and smoke at God's descent
the consuming fire of God's holiness (Deuteronomy 4:24; Hebrews 12:29)
the earth trembling at God's coming (Psalm 68:8; Habakkuk 3:6)

schools.summary: The trumpet and the dialogue of Moses and God: 'and when the voice of the trumpet sounded long, and waxed louder and louder, Moses spake, and God answered him by a voice' — the trumpet sounding ever louder, and Moses speaking, and God answering him by a voice (the supernatural trumpet, waxing louder, the awful herald of God's presence, vv.16, the dialogue, Moses speaking, God answering audibly, the confirmation, God speaking with Moses in the people's hearing, v.9); the trumpet growing louder (the supernatural sound, the herald of the theophany) — the awful summons (the increasing terror); 'Moses spake, and God answered him by a voice' (Moses speaking and God answering audibly, the people hearing God speak with Moses, confirming his mission, v.9, the dialogue, the mediator addressed by God in the hearing of the people, the providence, the confirmation of Moses, the giving of the law, ch. 20) — JFB, Gill.

themes: And when the voice of the trumpet sounded long, and waxed louder and louder, Moses spake, and God answered him by a voice
the trumpet and the dialogue of Moses and God
the supernatural trumpet waxing louder, the herald of God's presence (vv.16)
God answering Moses audibly, confirming the mediator (v.9)

schools.summary: The LORD calling Moses up the mount: 'and the LORD came down upon mount Sinai, on the top of the mount: and the LORD called Moses up to the top of the mount; and Moses went up' — the LORD coming down on the top of Sinai and calling Moses up to him (the descent of God and the ascent of the mediator, God coming down, Moses going up to meet him, the meeting on the mount, Moses admitted to God's presence, while the people stood below, the office, Moses the mediator, going up to God); the LORD descending on the top of the mount (the theophany, vv.18) — the presence of God on the mount; 'the LORD called Moses up to the top of the mount; and Moses went up' (Moses called up to God, the mediator admitted to the presence, while the people kept below, the office, Moses going up, a type of the Mediator, Christ, who alone goes up to God for his people, Hebrews 9:24, the providence, Moses receiving the further charge, vv.21-24, and the law, ch. 20) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the LORD came down upon mount Sinai, on the top of the mount: and the LORD called Moses up to the top of the mount; and Moses went up
the LORD calling Moses up the mount
God descending, the mediator going up to meet him
Moses a type of Christ, who alone goes up to God for his people (Hebrews 9:24)

schools.summary: The warning lest the people break through to gaze: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Go down, charge the people, lest they break through unto the LORD to gaze, and many of them perish' — God sending Moses down to warn the people not to break through the bounds (v.12) to gaze on God (out of curiosity or presumption), lest many perish (the renewed warning, the bounds enforced, the danger of presumption, the people's curiosity a peril, the holiness that destroys the presumptuous); the danger of breaking through to gaze (the presumptuous curiosity, the rash approach to the holy) — the warning against presumption (the reverence required); 'and many of them perish' (the death that would follow the presumption, the holiness that consumes the rash, v.12; Leviticus 10:1-3, the strange fire; 1 Samuel 6:19, the men of Bethshemesh, the terror of the holiness, the providence, the warning preserving the people, the reverence enforced) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Go down, charge the people, lest they break through unto the LORD to gaze, and many of them perish
the warning lest the people break through to gaze
the danger of presumption, the rash approach to the holy (v.12)
the holiness that consumes the presumptuous (Leviticus 10:1-3; 1 Samuel 6:19)

schools.summary: Even the priests to sanctify themselves: 'and let the priests also, which come near to the LORD, sanctify themselves, lest the LORD break forth upon them' — even the priests (those who came near to God, whether the firstborn, or the future Aaronic priests, or the elders) must sanctify themselves, lest the LORD break forth (in judgment) upon them (the holiness required of all, even those who draw near, the priests, must be consecrated, the danger, the holiness that destroys even the priests if they presume); the priests (those who draw near) charged to sanctify themselves — the holiness required even of the nearest (the lesson, those who minister before God must be holy, Leviticus 10:3, 'I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me'); 'lest the LORD break forth upon them' (the danger of the LORD breaking forth, in judgment, on the unsanctified, even the priests, 2 Samuel 6:7-8, Uzzah; Leviticus 10:1-2, Nadab and Abihu, the terror of the holiness, the providence, the warning, the reverence required even of those who draw near) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And let the priests also, which come near to the LORD, sanctify themselves, lest the LORD break forth upon them
even the priests to sanctify themselves
the holiness required of all who draw near to God (Leviticus 10:3)
the danger of the LORD breaking forth on the unsanctified (2 Samuel 6:7; Leviticus 10:1-2)

schools.summary: Moses citing the bounds already set: 'and Moses said unto the LORD, The people cannot come up to mount Sinai: for thou chargedst us, saying, Set bounds about the mount, and sanctify it' — Moses replying that the people could not come up, for God had already commanded the bounds to be set (v.12, the citing of the command, Moses noting that the barrier was in place, the reverence, Moses appealing to God's own charge, the obedience already done); Moses noting the people kept back (by the bounds already set, v.12) — the barrier in place (the obedience); 'Set bounds about the mount, and sanctify it' (the command already given, v.12, the mount sanctified, set apart, fenced, the providence, God renewing the charge, vv.24, that Moses might impress the warning the more, the reverence doubly enforced) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Moses said unto the LORD, The people cannot come up to mount Sinai: for thou chargedst us, saying, Set bounds about the mount, and sanctify it
Moses citing the bounds already set
the barrier in place, the command already obeyed (v.12)
the reverence enforced, the mount sanctified

schools.summary: The renewed charge and the distinction of Moses and Aaron: 'and the LORD said unto him, Away, get thee down, and thou shalt come up, thou, and Aaron with thee: but let not the priests and the people break through to come up unto the LORD, lest he break forth upon them' — God sending Moses down again (to warn the people once more), but granting that Moses (and Aaron with him) might come up (the renewed charge, the warning repeated, the distinction, Moses and Aaron admitted, the people and priests kept back, the office, the mediators allowed near, the people held at a distance); Moses and Aaron alone admitted (the mediators, the rest kept back) — the distinction (the few drawn near, the many held off, the office of the mediators); 'let not the priests and the people break through to come up unto the LORD, lest he break forth upon them' (the renewed warning, the bounds enforced, even the priests kept back, vv.21-22, the danger, the holiness that breaks forth on the presumptuous, the providence, the reverence doubly enforced, the distance kept, until the Mediator should open the way, Hebrews 10:19-20) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And the LORD said unto him, Away, get thee down, and thou shalt come up, thou, and Aaron with thee: but let not the priests and the people break through to come up unto the LORD, lest he break forth upon them
the renewed charge and the distinction of Moses and Aaron
the mediators admitted near, the people kept at a distance (vv.21-22)
the distance kept until the Mediator opens the way (Hebrews 10:19-20)

schools.summary: Moses going down to charge the people: 'so Moses went down unto the people, and spake unto them' — Moses obeying, going down to the people and speaking to them (the warning, the charge, vv.21-24, the carrying-out of the command, the mediator going down, to warn and prepare the people, the preparation completed before the giving of the law, ch. 20); Moses going down (obeying God's charge, v.24) — the obedience (the mediator descending to the people); 'and spake unto them' (Moses speaking to the people, delivering the warning, the charge, vv.21-24, the preparation, the people warned, the bounds enforced, the providence, the people made ready, the giving of the Ten Commandments, ch. 20) — Gill.

themes: So Moses went down unto the people, and spake unto them
Moses going down to charge the people
the mediator warning the people, the preparation completed (vv.21-24)
before the giving of the Ten Commandments (ch. 20)
